summary: DHH responds to criticism that Active Record is limited by MySQL's lack of advanced database features. He argues this isn't a constraint but a deliberate design opinion: business logic belongs in the domain model, not the database. He rejects stored procedures and database constraints as violations of object-oriented encapsulation. He distinguishes between application and integration databases, noting his stance applies to the former, and warns that expecting Rails to embrace enterprise database features is a losing bet.
key_points: Active Record's simplicity is an intentional design opinion, not a limitation imposed by MySQL
Stored procedures and database constraints destroy coherence by splitting business logic across layers
Software should have a single layer of cleverness — the domain model — rather than distributing logic into the database
The distinction between application databases and integration databases is critical to understanding this position
Rails will not change course to embrace enterprise database features regardless of MySQL's evolution
money_quotes: I don't want my database to be clever!
I consider stored procedures and constraints vile and reckless destroyers of coherence.
No, Mr. Database, you can not have my business logic. Your procedural ambitions will bear no fruit and you'll have to pry that logic from my dead, cold object-oriented hands.
I want a single layer of cleverness: My domain model.
Letting it sieve half ways through to the database is a terrible violation of those fine intentions.
Doing integration through the database belongs in a time where Beverly Hills 90210 was a hit show on TV.
key_insight: Good software architecture demands a single layer of cleverness — the domain model — and letting business logic leak into the database destroys the coherence that object-orientation was designed to provide.
